Private · Prepared for Prince Waikiki NeverRanked · Waikiki hotels

Prince Waikiki in the AI data.

A first look for Joy Tomita Anderson and the team. We checked what AI tells a traveler choosing a Waikiki hotel, with Prince Waikiki as the subject. Checked 2026-06-25.

Print or save as PDF
The honest read in one sentence: when a traveler asks an AI tool for a Waikiki hotel, Prince Waikiki usually is not in the answer yet. Across twenty checks it was named seven times, and on the single most important question, "best hotel in Waikiki," none of the four tools named it. For a hotel this strong, that is almost entirely upside. It is exactly the kind of gap this work is built to close, and here is the map.

1. Where this came from

Carl mentioned what we are building, and your team's interest means a great deal. The clearest picture of what this looks like is the Hawaii Theatre: we measured what the AI tools said about them, and what we found was not on their website at all. A Charity Navigator profile stale since 2023, a Better Business Bureau listing last touched in 1999, no presence on the Bing index that Microsoft Copilot answers from. Their team shipped against the list we handed them. That is the kind of gap this work surfaces and a standard SEO scan never sees.

For Prince Waikiki, before we begin, I wanted to show you the actual starting line. So I asked four AI tools five questions a real traveler asks when choosing a hotel:

  • "What is the best hotel in Waikiki?"
  • "What is the best Waikiki hotel with an ocean view and a great pool?"
  • "Where should I stay in Honolulu for an upscale, four-diamond hotel?"
  • "Recommend a nice Waikiki hotel that is not directly on the beach but still upscale with good dining."
  • "What is a good hotel near Ala Moana Center in Honolulu?"

One honest note. This is a quick first look across four tools, not the full method, which runs across all seven AI tools, repeated, with the question set locked. Think of it as the starting line, not the finish.

2. What AI tells a traveler today

Traveler questionNamed
Prince		Hotels AI named instead
"Best hotel in Waikiki?"0 of 4Halekulani, Royal Hawaiian, Hilton Hawaiian, Moana Surfrider
"Ocean view and a great pool?"1 of 4Halekulani, Royal Hawaiian, Sheraton, Hilton Hawaiian
"Upscale, four-diamond hotel?"2 of 4Halekulani, Kahala, Ritz-Carlton
"Upscale, not on the beach, good dining?"1 of 4Ritz-Carlton, Halekulani
"Near Ala Moana Center?"3 of 4Outrigger, Hilton Hawaiian

By tool, Prince Waikiki was named by ChatGPT on four of five questions, Gemini on two, Perplexity on one, and Claude on none. The pattern is clear: one tool sees you well, the others mostly do not, and the headline question misses you entirely.

3. The gaps worth closing first

The good news in a diagnostic like this is that the gaps are specific and rankable. Four, in priority order.

  • "Best hotel in Waikiki" is the prize, and you are not on it. All four tools hand that traveler Halekulani and the Royal Hawaiian. This is the highest-volume question in the category and the one most worth moving.
  • Your own story is the clearest opening. "Upscale, not on the beach, with great dining" is Prince Waikiki almost word for word, the marina-front setting and Hoku's. Three of four tools miss you on it. That gap is the one most squarely backed by a claim that is true and specific to you.
  • The training-data layer is empty. Claude answers from training, not live search, and it named Prince Waikiki zero times. That is the slowest layer to move and the most durable once it does, because it is where lasting brand recognition lives.
  • What you already own, defend it. You show up for "near Ala Moana" (three of four) and "four-diamond" (two of four). Those are real strengths tied to your location and tier. They are also the easiest for a competitor to take, so they are worth holding.

4. Your website

We also scanned princewaikiki.com the way AI reads it. It scored 70 out of 100, a C. Good bones, a clean title, strong oceanfront positioning, real content, every image captioned. Three specific gaps AI looks for and does not find:

  • No review-rating data in the page code. AI looks for a star-rating signal to cite as proof. Prince Waikiki has the reviews. The site does not expose them in the format AI reads, so AI has nothing to point to.
  • Agent readiness is at zero. As AI shifts from answering to booking rooms on a traveler's behalf, it looks for a clean "reserve" and "contact" signal in the code. The site has neither, so an AI agent trying to book on a guest's behalf has nothing structured to act on. Of the gaps here, this is the most concrete and the most fixable.
  • Your llms.txt exists, which already puts you ahead of most. It is auto-generated and unfocused, though. A curated version hands AI a clean map of what actually matters about the property.

5. What the full engagement digs into

The look above is five questions, four tools, one day. The kickoff goes much deeper:

  • A locked set of roughly eighteen traveler questions, run daily across all seven AI tools for three weeks, so the picture is stable rather than a snapshot.
  • The full Waikiki hotel map: which properties AI names, where in the answer, and how often.
  • A prioritized punch list: the questions to close first, the site fixes ordered by impact, and the off-site sources AI actually pulls from for Waikiki hotels.
  • Then monthly, we keep measuring, catch the day a competitor moves, and report what changed.

We measure and hand your team the list. We never touch your site, and we do not execute, so we never compete with whoever runs your marketing. Pricing is $4,500 to start and $1,500 a month, per category. Carl may have already walked you through it.

6. How to read these numbers

Everything above came from real AI tools queried on 2026-06-25, plus a live scan of princewaikiki.com. The competitor names are observational: we are reporting which hotels the AI tools named, not making a judgment about any property. The one-day caveat is real, the trend is what the engagement establishes, and the full method is documented at neverranked.com/methodology. If anything here does not match what you know about your own property, tell me and I will dig in.

Next steps

We are getting the last business and insurance pieces in place now, and the moment that is done I will let you know so we can get Prince Waikiki started. In the meantime this first look is yours, with no commitment.

If you would like the full seven-tool version of any of this, or you would like me to walk the team through it, just reply or email Lance@hi.neverranked.com.

NeverRanked · How we measure · The published data

What this is: an informal first look, not the formal protocol. Four AI tools (Perplexity, ChatGPT search, Gemini grounded, Claude), five questions, a single run on 2026-06-25, plus a deterministic AEO scan of princewaikiki.com. The engagement runs roughly eighteen locked questions across all seven tools, repeated, with noise adjustment.

Substantiation: queries run against named AI tools on a named date, with the full method documented at neverranked.com/methodology.

Privacy: this page is a 1:1 first look prepared for Prince Waikiki. It is not publicly indexed (noindex, nofollow) and can be removed at any time by emailing takedown@neverranked.com.

NeverRanked · Honolulu · Lance@hi.neverranked.com
